runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment for prime-age U.S. workers already working: percent change in annual hours worked from a 1% increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed and excluding participation responses.
citations: Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) labor supply survey
Heckman (1993) on labor supply elasticities
Saez, Slemrod & Giertz (2012) on taxable income/labor supply elasticities (background anchor)
runs.reasoningSummary: My prior is that intensive-margin hours responses for prime-age employed workers are small and slightly negative: higher non-labor income lowers desired hours modestly, but the effect is limited because the target excludes participation and uses annual hours conditional on working. I allow some near-zero or mildly positive mass because measurement error, heterogeneity, and wage-income interactions can blur the sign.

I allow some near-zero or mildly positive mass because measurement error, heterogeneity, and wage-income interactions can blur the sign."}

runs.reasoningSummary: For employed prime-age workers, I expect the intensive-margin income elasticity of hours to be small and slightly negative: higher non-labor income reduces hours a bit, but most of the large labor-supply response comes from participation rather than hours. I allow modest positive values in the upper tail because estimates can be noisy and close to zero.
